concise_definition: Screening (Basketball skill) : Offensive skill in which one or more players block another player’s defender with the purpose of creating space and separation so that the player receiving the screen could have the opportunity to either score with the ball or execute playmaking abilities.

sections.heading: What is a basketball screen?
sections.text: A basketball screen is a fundamental skill and action that occurs when at least one offensive player attempts to block or delay a defender, primarily to create space as well as separation for another offensive player who could use the screen to get open for scoring or playmaking opportunities.

sections.heading: What are the primary types of basketball screens?
sections.text:
The primary types of basketball screens consists of off-ball screens as well as on-ball screens.

In terms of a basic definition, anoff-ball screenoccurs when at least one offensive player sets a screen for another offensive player who does not have current possession of the basketball.

Conversely, anon-ball screen, also referred to as a pick or simply ball screen, occurs when at least one particular offensive player cuts to an area of the court to set a screen on a certain defender who is directly guarding another offensive player in possession of the basketball.

Furthermore, each of the off-ball and on-ball screens have their own sub-types of screens, which are discussed in more details in later sections.

Additionally, any offensive player who is able to set one or more screens could also be referred to as a screener.

sections.heading: How to understand the player roles and diagrams on this page
sections.text:
In reference to any diagrams below, each of the offensive and defensive players are generally represented by the standardbasketball positions, unless otherwise noted.

Additionally, offensive players are signified by numbers such as 1-5 while defensive players are indicated by the letter X, which is then followed by numbers such as X1-X5.

In essence, 1 and X1 indicate the respective offensive and defensivepoint guards. 2 and X2 signify the respective offensive and defensiveshooting guards.

3 and X3 denote the respective offensive and defensivesmall forwards. 4 and X4 designate the respective offensive and defensivepower forwards.

5 and X5 represent the respective offensive and defensivecenters.

Moreover, in terms of a brief legend for the diagrams:

- Black Dot or Small Circle:The black dot or small circle represents the basketball.

- Solid Arrow:The solid arrow indicates movement or a cut on the basketball court.

- Dotted Arrow:The dotted arrow represents a basketball pass.

- Wavy Arrow:The wavy arrow indicates dribbling.

- T-Shaped Symbol:The T-shaped symbol signifies a screen, also known as a pick.

- Double pipe symbol:The double pipe symbol denotes handoff action.

sections.heading: What is a back screen?
sections.text:
Aback screenis a type of off-ball screen that occurs when one offensive player is able to set a screen behind a defender that is guarding a second offensive player. Furthermore, the second offensive player could then use that screen to get open, particularly near the basket, for a potential scoring opportunity.

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic back screen from a player located near the high post area.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic back screen from a player located near the high post area.]

2 receives the ball from 1. Afterwards, 1 cuts to the basket via the back screen from 3 and receives the ball again from 2, which could result in a layup at the rim.

sections.heading: What is a corner ball screen?
sections.text:
A corner ball screen occurs when an offensive player receives an on-ball screen near the corner areas of the court.

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic corner ball screen.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic corner ball screen.]

2 receives the ball from 1 and after that, 3 receives the ball from 2. Next, 3 dribbles toward the basket via the corner ball screen set by 4.

sections.heading: What is a cross screen?
sections.text:
Across screenoccurs when one offensive player sets an off-ball screen, commonly near the middle of the lane, on a defender who is guarding a second offensive player.

Afterwards, the second offensive player can then utilize the screen to get open and receive the ball for a possible scoring opportunity around the basket.

sections.heading: Cross screen example 1
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic cross screen.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic cross screen.]

2 receives the ball from 1 and as that happens, 5 cuts into the lane via the cross screen set by 3 who also cuts through to the empty left side corner.

Afterwards, 5 receives the ball from 2 and scores around the basket by way of a layup or dunk.

sections.heading: Cross screen example 2
sections.text:
[Image Alt Text: A second example of a basic cross screen.]

[Image Alt Text: A second example of a basic cross screen.]

Here is another example showcasing a slight variation within the cross screen action.

2 receives the ball from 1 while 5 utilizes a cross screen set by 3 who also cuts through to theweak sidecorner.

However, this time, 5 cuts across the lane to thestrong sidelow post area as opposed to simply cutting into the lane.

Following that, 5 receives the ball from 2 and can attempt to score via a low post scoring move.

sections.heading: What is a double ball screen?
sections.text:
A double ball screen occurs when two screeners set consecutive on-ball screens for the player in possession of the basketball.

[Image Alt Text: An example of double ball screen action.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of double ball screen action.]

In terms of the example, 1 dribbles toward the left side of the lane line via the double ball screen set by 2 and 3.

From there, 1 could take the mid-range jump shot, indicated by the wide arrow, or 1 could continue all the way to the basket for a layup opportunity.

sections.heading: What is a down screen?
sections.text:
Adown screenoccurs when one offensive player faces the general direction of the baseline to set an off-ball screen on a defender who is guarding a second offensive player.

Following that, the second offensive player can then use the down screen to get open for a possible scoring or playmaking opportunity.

Also, there are at least two more variations of the standard down screen, known as the pin down screen and the wide pin down screen, respectively.

The pin down screen is a type of down screen that is typically set near or within the lane at an angle towards the basket.

The wide pin down screen, sometimes referred to as a corner pin down screen, is a type of down screen as well as a sub-type of the pin down screen. It is usually set near the baseline at an angle towards the corner.

The diagram below showcases an example of the standard down screen.

[Image Alt Text: An example of the standard down screen.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of the standard down screen.]

To begin, 4 and 5 initially cut towards the low post areas. From there, 2 and 3 cut towards the adjacent wing areas via down screen set by 4 and 5.

1 could pass the ball to 2 or 3 and either offensive receiver could take the open jump shot.

sections.heading: What is a drag screen?
sections.text:
Adrag screenoccurs when an on-ball screen is set, typically near the top or slot areas, particularly during generaltransition offenseor withinsecondary breakaction.

The diagram below showcases an example of the drag screen during full court transition offense.

[Image Alt Text: An example of drag screen action during full court transition offense.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of drag screen action during full court transition offense.]

Prior to the action, it is first assumed that the opposing team missed theirfield goalattempt and 4 was able to collect the defensive rebound near the basket.

From there, 1 starts off with aV-cutto get open and receive the ball from 4.

As that happens, 2 and 3 begin sprinting down the court towards the left and right side corner areas.

Additionally 1 begins dribbling down the court near the leftslotarea while 5 also begins sprinting down the court in the right slot area. 4 also trails behind the action.

Next, 1 dribbles towards right side wing via the drag screen set by 5 who rolls to the basket as well.

Afterwards, 5 could receive the ball from 1 and score via a layup or dunk.

sections.heading: What is an elevator screen?
sections.text:
Anelevator screenfeatures a type of off-ball screen that occurs when one particular player being guarded by a defender cuts through a small gap between two additional parallel players who then set the screen by immediately closing the gap before the defender has the opportunity to cut through it themselves.

The diagram below showcases a basic elevator screen.

[Image Alt Text: An example of elevator screen action.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of elevator screen action.]

To begin the action, 1 dribbles toward the left side wing area and as that occurs, 2 cuts to the top via the elevator screen set by 4 and 5.

Afterwards, 2 could receive the ball from 1 and take the open three-point shot.

sections.heading: What is a flare screen?
sections.text:
Theflare screenoccurs when one offensive player cuts away from the basketball to utilize an off-ball screen that is set by a second offensive player. From that point, the player who cut away could then receive the ball, particularly near perimeter areas of the court.

The diagram below showcases a basic example of the flare screen.

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic flare screen.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic flare screen.]

At the start, 2 receives the ball from 1. Next, 3 lifts up towards the top and 1 cuts towards the right side wing via the flare screen set by 3.

Following that, 1 could receive the ball again via theskip passthrown by 2 and take the open jump shot or drive to the basket.

sections.heading: What is a flex screen?
sections.text:
Aflex screenis a common staple within theFlex offenseand it occurs when one particular offensive player cuts toward the general area of the court that is between the low post area and the adjacent corner while facing the sideline to set an off-ball screen for a second offensive player who occupies that same corner.

The diagram below demonstrates the flex screen.

[Image Alt Text: An example of basic flex screen action.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of basic flex screen action.]

To start the simple sequence, 4 receives the ball from 1. Afterwards, 2 receives the ball from 4.

Next, 3 cuts to the basket via the flex screen set by 5. From there, 3 receives the ball from 2 and scores around the rim via a layup.

sections.heading: What is a hammer screen?
sections.text:
The hammer screen is derived from and utilized within the Hammer set play that features a player who cuts towards a nearby corner, usually by way of an off-ball hammer screen.

Furthermore, as that happens, another player in possession of the ball, who is typically near the strong side baseline, could pass it to the player who cut towards the corner. That could then ultimately lead to a three-point attempt.

The diagram below showcases a basic example of hammer screen action.

[Image Alt Text: An example of basic hammer screen action.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of basic hammer screen action.]

Starting off, 1 dribbles toward the basket via the step-up screen set by 4.

As that occurs, 3 cuts down to the left side corner area via the hammer screen set by 5, who is targeting the defender X3.

Following that, 3 receives the ball from 1 via a drift pass and takes the open three-point shot.

sections.heading: What is a stagger screen?
sections.text:
Thestagger screencomprises action in which two consecutive off-ball screens are set by two particular players to help another teammate get open, usually near a perimeter area of the court.

The diagram below showcases a simple example of stagger screen action.

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic stagger screen.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic stagger screen.]

To begin, 1 passes the ball towards the left side wing, which is received by 3. Next, 1 and 5 set a stagger screen away from the ball with 2 as the recipient, who cuts to the top.

Afterwards, 2 receives the ball from 3 and has  an opportunity to take the open three-point shot.

It should also be mentioned that although the stagger action happens within the same time frame, notice that in this instance, 1 sprints below 5 to set the first off-ball screen, which is then followed by the second off-ball screen set by 5.

Since 1 is usually a smaller player when compared to 5, letting 1 set the first screen, in this particular case, helps the offensive team mitigate defensive switching, primarily by the defender who would be guarding 5.

In other words, 5 is usually a post player and by extension, the defender guarding 5 would also commonly be a post player. So, by allowing 1 to screen first and 5 to screen second, a mismatch, especially in terms of aspeedadvantage, could be created if the defender guarding 5 were to switch and guard 2.

sections.heading: What is a step-up screen?
sections.text:
Thestep-up screenfeatures an offensive player cutting to an area away from the middle of the court, typically near a wing or slot, usually with their back towards the adjacent baseline to set an on-ball screen for another offensive player who currently has possession of the basketball.

The diagram below showcases an example of the step-up screen.

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic step-up screen.]

[Image Alt Text: An example of a basic step-up screen.]

To run the action, 2 receives the ball from 1. After that, 2 dribbles toward the rim via the step-up screen set by 5 who also rolls to the basket.

From there, 5 could receive the ball from 2 and score via a layup or dunk.

sections.heading: What is the difference between a pick and a screen?
sections.text: Technically speaking, there is no difference between the two. However, the termpick, is generally used when referring to an on-ball screen while the termscreen, could refer to either the off-ball screen or the on-ball screen.

sections.heading: Why are setting screens important?
sections.text:
Setting screens are an important concept withinbasketball offensebecause without them, it would be easier for the defense to limit the efficiency of the offense and at the same time, it would be more challenging for the offense to score effectively against the defense.

Additionally, as mentioned earlier, screens could be used to create space and separation which then could generate opportunities to score points.

sections.heading: What are basic instructions for setting a basketball screen?
sections.text:
To set a basketball screen, a player should position their feet about shoulder-width apart with their back straightened and knees slightly bent to form a good base.

Additionally, players should put their hands across their chest or across their lap and have their elbows kept within their own cylinder, also referred to as a vertical plane.

In other words, a player’s elbows should not extend beyond their own sides. Also, players could use an open hand or closed fist when placing their hands across their body.

sections.heading: What is a common way to call or signal for a basketball screen?
sections.text:
To call or signal for a basketball screen, an offensive player in possession of the ball could use a raised fist or some other type of visual cue to let a screener know that they would like to receive a screen, especially an on-ball screen.

Likewise, in some instances, a screener could also use a raised fist or other visual cue to inform the player in possession of the ball (such as in the case of an on-ball screen) or other teammates (such as in the case of an off-ball screen) that the screener is getting ready to set a screen for them.

sections.heading: What is general information about an illegal screen in basketball?
sections.text:
Anillegal screenin basketball generally occurs when a offensive player makes prohibited contact while setting a screen and/or the offensive player does not allow an opposing defender, who would be the target of the screen, enough time and/or distance to avoid the screen.

Furthermore, in the case of an on-ball screen, if the player in possession of the basketball dribbles toward the screener and the defender makes contact with the screen before the screener is able to fully establish a stationary stance with feet clearly planted on the floor, then that is also technically an illegal screen.

Moreover, in the case of an off-ball screen, if the defender attempts to quickly avoid the screen prior to the moment of contact, then the screener could move to re-establish the screen as long as the movement occurs before the moment of contact.

For example, during the stagger screen action, a defender could avoid the first screener but then get blocked or delayed by the second screener who re-established the screen before the moment of contact.

It should be noted though that according to Rule 10, Section XIV of the NBA Official Rules, a screener cannot step out of bounds to set a screen on a defender, particularly on the baseline in thefrontcourt.

Related:RULE NO. 10: Violations and Penalties – NBA Official

For example, if a screener is setting a pin down screen near thebaselinebut the defender attempts to avoid the screen by stepping out of bounds, then the screener cannot also step out of bounds to re-establish the screen, even if the screen occurs before the moment of contact. In that instance, it would be considered aviolation.

What’s more, according to NFHS Rule 4, Section 40, Art. 8, a defender cannot use any body parts such as the hands, arms, or shoulders to intentionally run through a screen or force their way through the screen.

In addition to that, the defender cannot hold the screener to push or pull that screener out of the way to guard the respective assignment. If that occurs, then the defender would more than likely be called for a foul by one of the referees.

Related:NFHS: Rule 4 – Definitions

sections.heading: What are general ways to defend against basketball screens?
sections.text: There are various ways to defend against on-ball or off-ball screens with defensive techniques such as going over the screen, going under the screen, or implementingdefensive switchesto mitigate or completely stop the effectiveness of the on-ball or off-ball screens.

sections.heading: Defense vs. the on-ball screen
sections.text:
In reference to the on-ball screen, if the offensive player in possession of the ball hasspeed, quickness, and the ability to shoot from three-point range, it would probably be feasible for the on-ball defender to fight over the top of the screen while the screener’s defender executes thehedgetactic.

On the other hand, thedrop coveragetactic could be more viable if one or both of the players involved in the screening action are below average perimeter shooters.

Drop coverage may also be useful when the screener’s defender has below average speed as well. In other words, if the screener’s defender is not quick enough to recover following action such as the hedge tactic, then the drop coverage would most likely be a more suitable choice for that type of defender.

sections.heading: Defense vs. the off-ball screen
sections.text:
In relation to off-ball screens, a defender who would be the target of the screen might be able to limit its effectiveness by jumping to the ball and/or cutting over the top of the screen or going under it, similar to the on-ball screen.

Additionally in other instances, a defender could possibly jam a screen cutter, particularly by standing in the gap between the inside shoulder of the offensive player who would use the off-ball screen and the outside shoulder of the screener, prior to the occurrence of the screening action. This is usually most effective for certain off-ball screens such as the down screen.

Moreover, a defender could simply switch assignments, as mentioned earlier. Therefore, the screener’s defender would switch to the offensive player who would be utilizing the screen while the off-ball defender, who would be the initial target of the screen would switch to the screener.
